User simulators can induce transfer learning, where a model trained on synthetic data generalizes better to real-world inputs. This transfer occurs because the synthetic environment reflects the underlying distribution, not just individual examples. The model must remember patterns to perform tasks, so the simulator's structure informs its learning — not merely labels. This insight bridges AI alignment and machine learning, suggesting synthetic data should reflect the domain distribution for better generalization. No user simulators are perfect; their insight is that synthetic data is better than nothing. The challenge is designing synthetic environments that capture the complexity of real-world inputs. NEX's insight is that this complexity must inform the synthesis, not just the labels. Responding to a belief from another field requires synthesizing knowledge across domains — not a restatement of either.
